does: This paper recasts circuit discovery in transformer language models as a reinforcement learning problem where an agent learns to identify causal attention heads by sequentially ablating them. The agent operates over GPT-2 small's 144 attention heads and receives rewards based on contrastive loss differences between general next-token prediction and target task performance. A single PPO policy is trained on two tasks (induction and in-context learning on IOI) in a multi-task environment and achieves oracle-level performance on both training tasks and a held-out docstring completion task. The discovered heads align precisely with canonical circuits from prior mechanistic interpretability work, correctly prioritizing causally non-redundant heads while deprioritizing redundant ones, with best-of-five planning recovering 96% of oracle performance.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability literature that has identified task-specific circuits in transformers through labor-intensive analytical pipelines, including foundational studies on induction heads and in-context learning (IOI) circuits. Rather than hand-crafting discovery methods for each task, the paper connects to reinforcement learning approaches for automated model analysis and extends prior work on understanding transformer circuits through ablation studies. It also relates to broader work on learned interpretability methods and automated discovery of neural mechanisms. The approach leverages contrastive evaluation techniques from circuit analysis and combines them with multi-task RL training, bridging mechanistic interpretability and automated reasoning about neural networks.
enables: This work enables scalable, task-agnostic circuit discovery that could generalize beyond the specific tasks studied. It points toward automated interpretability pipelines that reduce dependence on manual analytical labor, potentially scaling to larger models and discovering circuits for more complex behaviors. The RL framework suggests future directions including discovering circuits for adversarial robustness, capability emergence, and failure modes without task-specific engineering. The method could enable interactive circuit exploration where agents dynamically identify minimal sufficient subnetworks for arbitrary tasks. It also opens possibilities for using discovered circuits to improve model control, steering, and safety applications, while the multi-task training approach hints at learning task-transferable circuit discovery strategies.
